author: @Ferd414
text: I had a somewhat similar project (Can't explicitly name it - Youtube would get all grumpy and delete my comment and/or put me in comment-jail if I did - suffice to say that long-distance hole punchers are involved) that was built around this same general concept. I couldn't get enough "oomph" out of the printed PLA coil spring the plans suggested/supplied the .STL for, so started looking around, and found that the half-inch (13-14mm) wide steel banding straps, like you might find holding together a bundle of lumber, or pallet of concrete blocks/bricks, can be had (frequently for the asking) from quite a few building-supply places as "shipping/packaging trash". I picked up about 80 feet of the stuff from my local Home Depot and used half a dozen turns of it as the spring for the project I was working on with EXCELLENT results. Advice: Grab the "cleanest" straps you can find - bent/twisted/kinked/creased pieces are pretty useless, but if they're at one end or the other of your piece, they can be snipped off, then you can bend your required hooks in the ends to anchor it using pliers and a vise, or whatever works for you. Advice 2: Whether you use a pre-formed "commercial" clock-spring/starter-rewind spring as discussed here, or improvise one with strapping like I'm speaking of, LUBRICATE BOTH FLATS OF THE SPRING BEFORE INSTALLING. Any halfway decent lube will do - 3-in-one oil, axle grease, powdered graphite, spray-on lithium grease, a dab of Tacky-Slick, if it can still be found in your area, a few drops of motor oil, a blob of vaseline/petroleum jelly, rub on a bit of candle wax or a crayon, or use a smear of lard, tallow, or even bacon grease - it will be better than nothing (remember, kids, no matter how popular it is, WD-40 *IS NOT REALLY A LUBRICANT*, even though it can serve as one in a short-term emergency - just be prepared for it to gum up, attract "gunk", and become more of an adhesive than a lubricant as time passes) and will *GREATLY* improve both the longevity of the device, AND the amount of power you can store in it/extract from it per wind-up. Go easy, though - you want it "lubed", but you don't want it "swimming in lube" - "More" isn't always "better", and this is a prime example of one of those cases.

author: @MrNatural451
text:
Is the whole motor printed in PLA? You mentioned that PLA tends to wear out and break. Is PLA still a good choice for the ratchet? 
In other news, another great video. You continue to stretch my brain. Thank you, it needs the exercise.

replies.author: @JD_Mortal
replies.text:
PLA is brittle, but strong. It's like a diamond, vs steel. It will become more brittle, in time, as it doesn't absorb moisture well.

Honestly, they make great ratchet pawls, but you should look at spring-set pawls, for better operation. It is better to have more pawls, offset on toothing, with a light spring to set them into position. That reduces wear and can yield greater catch positioning. As long as there are enough pawls per catch position, to hold the load.

That "V" design is not ideal, in any way. It should have been a simple, single curve, transferring force to the center. A set of three "(" pawls.

Google ratchet drives, and you can find many easy ones to build, which are time-tried successes, not artistic creations, untested in time. 😉

author: @ThatRobHuman
text:
I've just happened across you're channel, and I've been enjoying it immensely. In particular, I really appreciate how jovial (despite your recent loss, my sincere condolences), and infectiously enthusiastic you are. I also appreciate how much you put in these "Meta" lessons. It's not just about building a flywheel, it's about thinking critically about your workflow to find efficiencies; it's not just about a pendulum escapement, it's about reducing a problem to its base components to shrink the problem space. You're a brilliant educator, and I'm really looking forward to seeing more.

As an aside, I've already started replacing many of my bearings in my 3d printed projects with 3d printed cylindrical bearings instead of buying them off amazon, thanks to you.

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: Do the springs come with a rating of how much energy they can store?  If not is there a way to calculate from first principles such as type of steel, width, number of turns etc....?  If not one can always do a practical test but that can be an expensive way to find out what spring you need for a given application.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate they don't and you could try it but chances are it would be wrong as there are quite a lot of variables and some of them out of your control - like temp for example

replies.author: @maxkennedy5073
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I had looked before asking and couldn't find a rating but thought I'd ask in case I'd missed something.  Thanks.

replies.author: @JD_Mortal
replies.text:
Rating depends on various things. Because these are designed for something specific, the rating is "adequate for intended operation". 😉

A pre-coiled spring-steel will have a much different rating in various setups. An uncoiled spring-steel, of the same length, will be completely different in ratings. Pre-coiling is done to provide more consistent output, vs one that is not pre-coiled. The expanded state, coil wind tightness, material hardness, and wound minimum, will all alter the output power and potential at various stages of unwinding and winding.

The most power will come from a new, unwound spring-steel flat. It will be hard to wind, unwind fast, may bind while unwinding and have the most inconsistent output. A high pre-wound coil will be easy to wind, not last as long, have less driving power, will not often bind when unwinding and will provide a more consistent output power torque.

If you can regulate output well and keep the coil well lubed, so it doesn't bind... An unwound flat of spring-steel will be your best source for power. However, due to constant winding and shape holding, in time, it will become partially wound and lose potential power, a bit. It'll easily outlast a pre-coiled spring though.

replies.author: @maxkennedy5073
replies.text: @JD_Mortal And all these springs are determined to be "adequate" by trial and error right?  There's no design principles involved.  Hmmmm, doesn't sound right.

replies.author: @STEM-ll9mc
replies.text:
Any reason why the design couldn't be altered, to allow several springs to operate side by side, so you use only the number of springs sufficient for your needs? 
Bought at the same time, the springs are likely to be from the same batch. A modular spring battery.  ✅

author: @FordPrefect23
text: Another cracking video. On the topic of coil springs, when printed from PETG they work quite well. I have a Prusa MK4S printer with the MMU3 and I really disliked the factory buffer system. So I replaced the whole thing with a set of printed spool rewinders that sit atop the enclosure, held in place by magnets. The rewinders are a stack of interlocking PETG coil springs held inside a tube that goes in the spool centre. One end of the stack locks into the outer tube and the other end is prevented from turning by a flat that matches a flat in a spindle passing through the entire stack. So as the spool pays out filament the springs are wound up, then during a filament change it winds backwards. So far I've done hundreds of hours of prints and they're still running well.

replies.author: @simonprecheurllarena
replies.text: The main drawback of pull string mechanisms, and why they're not used in clocks, watches, and other high quality mechanical designs, is in the name itself - the strings are made of materials that usually break up after a few years. The premise of these mechanical objects is to try and make them last centuries, which is why they're usually made of metal, at least in my field. I'm sure that for a radio that's planned for 10 years usage that could be a solution. Cheers!

The Train - A.A. Milne

Let it rain, who cares?
I've a train -- upstairs,
With a brake that I make from a string sorta thing --
Which works -- in jerks,
'Cause it drops in the spring and it stops with the string,
And the wheels all stick so quick that it feels
Like a thing that I make with a brake, not string.
Let it rain, -- who cares?I've a train -- upstairs,
With a brake that I make from a string sorta thing --
